steps.title: Fastest path: use angle relationships first
steps.content: Use the given angle relationships first: equal angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are organized.

steps.title: Recognize two pairs of corresponding angles are already equal
steps.content: We are given $\angle J = \angle X = 51^{\circ}$ and $\angle K = \angle Y = 89^{\circ}$. Under the natural correspondence ($J \leftrightarrow X$, $K \leftrightarrow Y$), we already have two pairs of corresponding angles that are congruent.

steps.title: Apply AA similarity criterion
steps.content:
The AA (Angle-Angle) criterion for similarity states: if two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent, the triangles are similar.

We already have this condition satisfied: $\angle J = \angle X$ and $\angle K = \angle Y$.

Therefore, $\triangle JKL \sim \triangle XYZ$ without needing to know the measures of angles $L$ and $Z$.

That confirms D is correct.
